# 📋 APPENDIX A: Regulatory Compliance Mapping
## Carte de Conformité Réglementaire / خريطة الامتثال التنظيمي
**Al-Madrasa Al-Shamila — Version 2.1 (Inspection-Grade)**

> **Document de référence pour inspections MENPS/AREF • وثيقة مرجعية لتفتيش الوزارة/الأكاديميات**
> *Last Updated: February 2026 | Confidential — For Regulatory Review*

---

## 1. Executive Compliance Statement
### Déclaration Exécutive de Conformité / البيان التنفيذي للامتثال

```
FRENCH:
L'École Al-Madrasa Al-Shamila s'engage à délivrer 100% du programme officiel 
du Ministère de l'Éducation Nationale, du Préscolaire et des Sports (MENPS) 
pour les niveaux Collège et Lycée, conformément aux dispositions de la Loi 51.17 
et aux circulaires d'application en vigueur. 

Toute différenciation pédagogique intervient exclusivement :
• Dans les méthodes d'enseignement (pédagogie structurée, intégration de valeurs)
• Dans les créneaux non obligatoires (vendredi après-midi, activités périscolaires)
• Dans l'accompagnement complémentaire (portfolios, projets, enrichissement)

Aucune substitution de contenu officiel n'est pratiquée.

ARABIC:
تلتزم مدرسة المدرسة الشاملة بتدريس 100% من المنهاج الرسمي لوزارة التربية الوطنية 
والتعليم الأولي والرياضة لمستويات الإعدادي والثانوي، وفقاً لأحكام القانون 51.17 
والمناشير التطبيقية السارية.

يتم أي تميز بيداغوجي حصراً من خلال:
• طرق التدريس (بيداغوجيا منظمة، دمج القيم)
• الفترات غير الإلزامية (بعد ظهر الجمعة، الأنشطة اللاصفية)
• الدعم التكميلي (ملفات الإنجاز، المشاريع، الإثراء)

لا يتم أي استبدال للمحتوى الرسمي.
```

---

## 2. Regulatory Reference Framework
### Cadre Réglementaire de Référence / الإطار التنظيمي المرجعي

| Reference / Référence | Title / Intitulé | Relevance to Shamila / Pertinence pour Shamila | Compliance Action / Action de conformité |
|---------------------|-----------------|----------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| **Loi 51.17** (2019) | Loi-cadre relative au système d'éducation, de formation et de recherche scientifique | Defines private school licensing, curriculum obligations, inspection authority | Full curriculum delivery; AREF registration; annual inspection readiness |
| **Draft Law 59.21** (2025) | Projet de loi relatif aux établissements d'enseignement privé (en cours d'adoption) | Proposed social quotas, fee transparency, contract requirements | Implement 15% free places target; written contracts; fee publication; monitor final text |
| **Circulaire MENPS 2025-09** | Mesures de régulation des frais de scolarité et contrats avec les familles | Mandatory written contracts; ban on mid-year fee increases; transparency | Standardized family contracts; published fee schedule; no mid-year modifications |
| **Arrêté ministériel (streams)** | Organisation des filières du Baccalauréat marocain | Defines Tronc Commun + specialization streams (Sciences, Lettres, Éco-Gestion) | Deliver all mandated subjects per stream; official timetables; Bac-format assessments |
| **AREF Guidelines** | Directives régionales pour l'agrément et le suivi des établissements privés | Regional inspection protocols; teacher qualification requirements; facility standards | Compliance Officer role; teacher file audits; facility safety documentation |
| **PSP Framework** (2023) | Programme Écoles Pionnières — structured pedagogy + TaRL remediation | Evidence-based teaching methods compatible with Ministry priorities | Integrate PSP-inspired structured lessons for core literacy/numeracy; document alignment |

> **Note de veille réglementaire / ملاحظة الرصد التنظيمي**: 
> *La conformité est révisée trimestriellement par le Responsable Conformité. Toute évolution législative (notamment Loi 59.21) fait l'objet d'une analyse d'impact sous 15 jours.*
> *الامتثال يُراجع فصلياً من قبل مسؤول الامتثال. أي تطور تشريعي (خاصة القانون 59.21) يخضع لتحليل الأثر خلال 15 يوماً.*

---

## 3. Subject Delivery Mapping: Collège (Grades 7-9)
### Cartographie de Livraison des Matières : Collège / خريطة تدريس المواد: الإعدادي

| Official Subject / Matière Officielle | Ministry Requirements / Exigences MENPS | Shamila Delivery Method / Méthode de livraison Shamila | Enrichment Layer / Couche d'enrichissement | Evidence Type / Type de preuve | Responsible / Responsable | Audit Frequency / Fréquence d'audit |
|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Langue Arabe** (6h/sem) | Programmes officiels; manuels approuvés; préparation Bac | Structured lessons (PSP-inspired); certified Arabic specialist; official textbooks | Quranic reflection starter (5 min); rhetorical analysis extension; debate club (after-school) | Lesson plans; attendance; mock exams; club logs | Arabic Dept. Head + Compliance Officer | Monthly lesson review; termly inspection pack |
| **Instruction Islamique** (2h/sem) | Programmes officiels; focus on values, citizenship | Official curriculum delivered by qualified teacher; Ministry-approved resources | Values integration across subjects; ethical dilemma discussions; service-learning projects | Curriculum maps; teacher qualifications; project documentation | Islamic Education Lead + Values Coordinator | Quarterly values integration audit |
| **Disciplines Sociales** (3h/sem) | History, Geography, Civics per national programs | Official content + critical questioning framework; primary source analysis | Community mapping projects; local history documentation; civic engagement clubs | Student projects; source analysis worksheets; club participation records | Social Studies Lead + Community Engagement | Termly project review |
| **Français** (6h/sem) | Language of instruction for sciences; official programs | Structured pedagogy; French specialist; scientific communication focus | Francophone media analysis; writing workshops; debate team (after-school) | Lesson plans; writing portfolios; competition participation | French Dept. Head + Literacy Coordinator | Monthly writing portfolio review |
| **Mathématiques** (6h/sem) | Official programs; problem-solving; Bac preparation | PSP-inspired structured lessons; Math specialist; regular formative assessments | Real-world application projects (budgeting, data analysis); math club; enterprise support | Assessment records; project rubrics; club logs | Math Dept. Head + Data Coordinator | Bi-weekly assessment review |
| **Sciences Naturelles** (2h/sem) | Official programs; lab work; environmental education | Official curriculum + inquiry-based labs; safety protocols | Environmental monitoring projects; health awareness campaigns; science fair | Lab reports; project documentation; safety logs | Science Coordinator + Lab Manager | Termly lab safety audit |
| **Sciences Physiques** (2h/sem) | Official programs; experimental approach | Structured lessons; equipment per Ministry standards | Renewable energy projects; physics applications club; innovation challenges | Experiment records; project outputs; equipment logs | Physics Specialist + Innovation Lead | Termly equipment + project audit |
| **Éducation Physique** (3h/sem) | Official programs; health, teamwork, sportsmanship | Ministry curriculum; certified PE teacher; inclusive activities | Wellness workshops; sports leadership training; inter-house competitions | Attendance; fitness assessments; competition records | PE Coordinator + Wellness Lead | Termly wellness review |
| **Éducation Plastique** (1h/sem) | Official programs; creativity, cultural expression | Official curriculum + values-integrated projects | Digital arts club; cultural heritage documentation; community art projects | Student portfolios; exhibition records; project documentation | Arts Coordinator + Cultural Lead | Annual portfolio showcase |
| **Initiation à la Technologie** (2h/sem) | Basic digital literacy; ICT skills per Ministry | Official ICT curriculum; offline-first tools; digital citizenship | AI literacy modules (opt-in); coding club; tech-for-good projects | Lesson plans; student work; club participation | Tech Coordinator + AI Ethics Lead | Termly digital citizenship review |

> **Timetable Conformity Note / Note de conformité emploi du temps**:
> *All core subjects delivered within Ministry-mandated weekly hours. Enrichment activities scheduled exclusively in Friday PM blocks (14:30-16:00) or after-school (16:00-18:00), with separate attendance tracking and no interference with core instruction.*
> *جميع المواد الأساسية تُدرّس ضمن الساعات الأسبوعية الإلزامية حسب الوزارة. أنشطة الإثراء تُجدول حصراً في فترات بعد ظهر الجمعة (14:30-16:00) أو بعد الدوام (16:00-18:00)، مع تتبع حضور منفصل وبدون تداخل مع التدريس الأساسي.*

---

## 4. Subject Delivery Mapping: Lycée Streams (Grades 10-12)
### Cartographie par Filière Lycée / الخريطة حسب شعب الثانوي

#### 4.1 Tronc Commun (Year 10) — All Streams
| Official Subject | Ministry Requirements | Shamila Delivery | Enrichment Layer | Evidence | Responsible | Audit |
|-----------------|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------|-------------|-------|
| **Arabe** (4h) | Official program; literary analysis; Bac prep | Structured lessons; specialist teacher; mock exams | Rhetorical analysis extension; media literacy club | Lesson plans; exam records; club logs | Arabic Lead | Monthly |
| **Français** (4h) | Scientific communication; Bac prep | Content-language integration; specialist | Writing workshops; debate team; Francophone culture club | Writing portfolios; competition records | French Lead | Monthly |
| **Anglais** (3h) | Optional enrichment; communication skills | Task-based learning; TOEFL/IELTS prep (opt-in) | AI literacy modules; global citizenship projects | Participation logs; portfolio artifacts | English Coordinator | Termly |
| **Mathématiques** (5h) | Official program; problem-solving | PSP-inspired structured pedagogy; regular assessments | Real-world applications; math club; enterprise support | Assessment records; project rubrics | Math Lead | Bi-weekly |
| **Physique-Chimie** (3h) | Official program; lab work | Inquiry-based labs; safety protocols | Renewable energy projects; innovation challenges | Lab reports; project documentation | Science Coordinator | Termly |
| **SVT** (2h) | Official program; environmental focus | Official curriculum + community applications | Environmental monitoring; health campaigns | Project records; safety logs | Biology Specialist | Termly |
| **Histoire-Géographie** (2h) | Official program; critical analysis | Primary source analysis; critical questioning | Community mapping; local history projects | Student projects; source worksheets | Social Studies Lead | Termly |
| **Éducation Islamique** (2h) | Official values/citizenship curriculum | Qualified teacher; Ministry resources | Values integration across subjects; service-learning | Curriculum maps; project documentation | Islamic Ed Lead | Quarterly |
| **Philosophie** (2h) | Introduced in Lycée; critical reasoning | Socratic seminars; ethical frameworks | Ethics bowl; AI ethics discussions | Seminar records; reflection journals | Philosophy Lead | Termly |
| **EPS** (2h) | Official program; health/teamwork | Certified teacher; inclusive activities | Wellness workshops; sports leadership | Attendance; fitness assessments | PE Coordinator | Termly |

#### 4.2 Specialization Streams (Years 11-12) — Example: Sciences Expérimentales
| Stream-Specific Subject | Ministry Requirements | Shamila Delivery | Enrichment Layer | Evidence | Responsible |
|------------------------|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------|-------------|
| **Mathématiques Avancées** (6h) | Advanced calculus; Bac exam prep | Specialist teacher; structured problem sets | Mathematical modeling projects; research mentorship | Problem sets; project rubrics; mentorship logs | Math Specialist |
| **Physique-Chimie Avancée** (5h) | Advanced topics; lab-intensive | Inquiry labs; safety protocols; Bac prep | Research projects; innovation challenges; industry partnerships | Lab reports; project documentation; partnership MOUs | Science Coordinator |
| **SVT Avancée** (4h) | Advanced biology; environmental focus | Official curriculum + community applications | Public health projects; environmental monitoring | Project records; community impact reports | Biology Specialist |
| **Français Scientifique** (2h) | Technical communication; scientific writing | Content-language integration; writing workshops | Research abstract writing; conference presentation prep | Writing portfolios; presentation records | French Lead + Science Coordinator |

> **Stream Conformity Note**: *Each specialization stream delivers 100% of Ministry-mandated content and hours. Enrichment activities are additive, scheduled in non-core time, and documented separately.*

---

## 5. Assessment & Baccalauréat Preparation Protocol
### Protocole d'Évaluation et Préparation au Bac / بروتوكول التقييم والتحضير للباكالوريا

| Component | Ministry Requirement | Shamila Implementation | Evidence Archive | Responsible |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Formative Assessment** | Regular evaluation per subject | Weekly quizzes; exit tickets; PSP-inspired checks | Digital gradebook; paper backups; termly reports | Subject Teachers + Academic Dean |
| **Mock Exams (Régional/National)** | Preparation for official Bac format | Quarterly mock exams using official rubrics; timed conditions | Exam papers; grading rubrics; student answer archives; analysis reports | Exam Coordinator + Compliance Officer |
| **Bac Registration & Logistics** | Official registration through AREF | Full administrative support; deadline tracking; candidate preparation | Registration confirmations; candidate lists; logistics checklists | Administrative Director + Compliance Officer |
| **Remediation (TaRL-inspired)** | Support for struggling students | Diagnostic assessments; flexible grouping; targeted interventions | Diagnostic records; intervention plans; progress tracking | Academic Dean + Subject Specialists |
| **Portfolio Documentation** (Supplementary) | Not required for Bac; optional for international | Student-curated portfolios for enrichment projects; values integration | Digital portfolios; rubric scores; reflection journals | Student Life Dean + Portfolio Coordinator |

> **Critical Compliance Boundary / Limite de conformité critique**:
> *Portfolios and enrichment assessments are NEVER used for official Bac grading or Ministry reporting. They are strictly supplementary for student development, international applications, or internal quality improvement.*
> *ملفات الإنجاز وتقييمات الإثراء لا تُستخدم أبداً في تقدير الباكالوريا الرسمي أو التقارير الوزارية. هي تكميلية حصراً لتطوير الطالب أو الطلبات الدولية أو تحسين الجودة الداخلي.*

## 7. Administrative Compliance: Contracts, Fees, Scholarships
### Conformité Administrative: Contrats, Frais, Bourses / الامتثال الإداري: العقود، الرسوم، المنح

| Requirement | Ministry/Regulatory Basis | Shamila Implementation | Evidence Type | Responsible |
|-------------|--------------------------|----------------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Written Family Contracts** | Circulaire MENPS 2025-09; Draft Law 59.21 | Standardized trilingual contract (Ar/Fr/En); clear fee schedule; no mid-year modifications; signature + copy to family | Signed contracts archive; fee schedule publication; amendment log (if any) | Administrative Director + Compliance Officer |
| **Fee Transparency** | Same as above | Published fee schedule on website + physical display; itemized breakdown; scholarship policy clearly stated | Website screenshots; physical display photos; scholarship policy document | Marketing Lead + Compliance Officer |
| **Scholarship Process (15% target)** | Draft Law 59.21 (proposed social quota) | Transparent application form; income verification; selection committee (Director + Community Rep + Academic Lead); annual review | Application forms; verification documents; committee minutes; selection records; retention tracking | Scholarship Coordinator + Compliance Officer |
| **No Mid-Year Fee Increases** | Circulaire 2025-09 | Fee schedule locked at enrollment; any exceptional changes require Ministry approval + 30-day family notice | Fee change log (empty if compliant); Ministry approval records (if applicable) | Finance Director + Compliance Officer |
| **Re-enrollment Policy** | Standard private school regulation | Clear timeline; academic standing criteria; scholarship renewal process; written communication to families | Policy document; communication templates; re-enrollment records | Academic Dean + Administrative Director |

> **Scholarship Process Flow / Processus de sélection des bourses**:
> ```
> 1. Application (Jan-Feb): Family submits form + income documentation
> 2. Verification (Mar): Administrative review + community reference check
> 3. Committee Review (Apr): Selection committee evaluates academic potential + need
> 4. Notification (May): Written decision to families; appeal process if declined
> 5. Annual Review (Jun): Progress check; renewal criteria communicated
> 6. Archive: All documents stored securely; data protection compliant
> ```

c357aaa9-6ea9-4194-8cf7-0514c15cec45.content:
The revised "Compliance-First Edition" (Version 2.0) is a **massive, necessary improvement** over the original revolutionary blueprint. You've correctly identified the non-negotiable legal wall: private lycées in Morocco **cannot legally replace or sideline** the official national curriculum and must prepare students for the national baccalauréat. The "Compliance + Layering" strategy is the only realistic path in 2025–2026 that avoids license denial, inspection failure, worthless diplomas, or zero enrollment.

That said, even this toned-down version still contains **several factual inaccuracies, optimistic assumptions, and remaining compliance risks**. I'll fact-check bluntly, highlight what's solid vs. what's still dangerously wrong or overstated, then give prioritized fixes.

### What You Got Right (Strong Parts)
- **Core diagnosis is accurate** — No "innovation waiver" exists for core secondary programs. Private schools must deliver 100% of the Ministry program (tronc commun + chosen stream), use approved resources where required, and center preparation on regional/national bac exams (scheduled for June 2026 in the current cycle).
- **Dual-track architecture** is the correct workaround: official morning/core hours for mandatory content + enrichment in Friday PM / after-school / embedded pedagogy.
- **15% mandatory scholarships / free places** aligns with real 2025 regulatory pressure (law 59.21 drafts and announcements emphasize social quotas for disadvantaged students in private establishments; some proposals even floated 30%+ splits, though final enforcement varies).
- **Written contracts, fee transparency, no mid-year increases** are now **mandatory** from 2025–2026 rentrée onward — you nailed this.
- **Bac pass rate target ≥92%** is ambitious but defensible as a marketing differentiator (national average hovers ~66–75% in recent years).
- **Offline-first AI, opt-in modules, transparency policies** reduce risk in a country with uneven connectivity and conservative parental attitudes.

### What Is Still Wrong or Over-Optimistic (Blunt Fact-Checks)
1. **No evidence that 15% is already a hard legal quota in 2025–2026**  
   Current texts (law 59.21 discussions, Ministry announcements May–Sep 2025) **push for** social inclusion / quotas in private schools, but it's not yet codified as a strict 15% obligation in final decrees. It's more aspirational/enforcement ramp-up than ironclad rule today. Calling it "mandatory per Ministry requirement" is premature — treat it as strong expectation / licensing pressure, not absolute law.

2. **Law 59.21 is still not fully in force in February 2026**  
   Adopted by government April 2025, examined in parliamentary commission May 2025 onward. As of early 2026, full implementation (especially private-sector sanctions, program conformity checks, equipment/teacher standards) remains partial/pending. Your document assumes everything is already binding — that's too aggressive. Risk: inspectors can still sanction under older rules or transitional provisions.

3. **Private schools face tighter — not looser — scrutiny on program fidelity**  
   Recent Ministry signals (2025) emphasize **conformity to official programs**, use of approved manuals, and administrative/pedagogical controls. "Modifying PSP materials for local context + values" sounds innocent but could trigger red flags if it looks like deviation. Inspectors target private establishments precisely for "non-conformity" issues.

4. **IB parallel track is mentioned casually but is very hard / expensive**  
   Adding optional IB Diploma authorization is realistic only for well-funded schools already operating internationally (e.g., American School of Marrakesh model). For a new Moroccan-bac-focused private lycée, pursuing IB from scratch in years 2–3 is financially and regulatorily heavy — IB requires separate authorization, fees, teacher training, and often English-medium delivery, clashing with Arabic/French primacy.

5. **Financial model remains too rosy**  
   30–40% margins by year 5, 32% IRR, rapid breakeven — these are startup fantasy numbers for Moroccan private education. Real mid-tier private lycées often run 10–25% margins after scholarships, staffing (30%+ of costs is realistic), facilities, and regulatory overhead. 15–25% scholarships will crush early cash flow more than modeled.

6. **English as "enrichment" is fine, but trilingual marketing overpromises**  
   Official Bac streams remain Arabic + French dominant (French especially in sciences/math). Heavy English/AI focus risks parent perception of "not serious about Bac" unless positioned very carefully.

### Prioritized Fixes – Make It Bullet-Proof
**Tier 1 – Immediate Legal / Survival Fixes (Do These First)**
- Replace every "mandatory 15%" with "**targeted 15% in line with Ministry social inclusion objectives and licensing expectations**" — avoid claiming it's already law.
- Add disclaimer everywhere: "**This model complies with regulations in force as of February 2026 and will be adapted to future decrees under law 59.21**."
- Strengthen compliance language: "All official subjects use Ministry-approved textbooks/resources where required; no content substitution occurs."
- Drop or heavily qualify the IB parallel track mention — replace with "exploratory feasibility for select internationally-oriented students in later phases."

**Tier 2 – Realism & Credibility Upgrades**
- Revise financial snapshot: cap year-5 margin at 20–28%, IRR at 18–25%, add explicit sensitivity for 20–30% scholarship drag.
- In assessment section: emphasize "**Bac remains the sole official certification**; portfolios strictly supplementary for international / personal use."
- Parent value proposition: lead with "**Guaranteed Bac preparation at elite levels + character & future-readiness development**" — Bac first, values second.
- Governance: make Compliance Officer report directly to Director with monthly risk logs — inspections are the #1 killer for new privates.

**Tier 3 – Polish & Differentiation**
- Sample schedule is solid — keep it, but add note: "Timetable respects Ministry-mandated weekly hours per subject/stream."
- AI section is appropriately cautious now — good.
- Systemic impact (public-school training) is smart leverage — keep, but frame as voluntary partnership, not guaranteed adoption.

### Bottom-Line Verdict (February 2026)
Version 2.0 is **now defensible and fundable** — you've pivoted from suicidal idealism to pragmatic ambition. With the fixes above (especially toning down quota certainty and financial hype), this becomes a credible business/pedagogical plan that could actually open and survive inspections.

It won't produce the revolutionary school of the original dream — but it **can** become one of the best private lycées in Morocco: high Bac results, strong values character-building, modest but meaningful enrichment, and real equity effort.

  
Draft the **Regulatory Compliance Mapping Appendix A** (Arabic/French version) showing exact crosswalk Ministry subjects → your delivery + enrichment layering. That's the document inspectors and investors will demand first.

- **Compliance Officer role** elevated to strategic importance
- **Ministry Liaison** on board—brilliant for regulatory navigation
- **Diverse perspectives** (education, industry, community, ethics, mental health)

**Enhancements:**

| Enhancement | Rationale |
|-------------|-----------|
| **Term limits** for board members | Ensures fresh perspectives; prevents entrenchment |
| **Succession planning** for key roles | Mitigates key-person risk |
| **Annual governance review** | Assesses board effectiveness; identifies gaps |
| **Student voice** on Advisory Council (already noted) | Ensure lived experience informs decisions |

---

### 8️⃣ Implementation Roadmap: **9.2/10** ✅

**Strengths:**
- **Clear phases** (Foundation → Enrichment → Maturity)
- **Milestone-driven** with success metrics
- **Regulatory deep-dive** prioritized in Month 1
- **Parent communication** built into early phases

**Enhancements:**

| Enhancement | Rationale |
|-------------|-----------|
| **Risk register** with mitigation strategies | Proactively addresses enrollment, regulatory, talent risks |
| **Contingency budget** (5-10% of startup costs) | Buffers against unforeseen challenges |
| **Quarterly review cadence** with clear decision points | Enables adaptive management |
| **Stakeholder communication plan** | Keeps parents, teachers, partners informed and engaged |

---

## 🔥 5 STRATEGIC UPGRADES FOR 9.8/10

### 1. Develop "Bac Success + Dimensions" Parent Dashboard
*Current gap: Parent communication is strong but not data-driven*

**Components:**
- **Real-time access** to Bac preparation progress (mock exam scores, subject mastery)
- **Dimensions participation tracking** (clubs attended, projects completed, skills developed)
- **Portfolio showcase** (optional sharing of student work)
- **Parent feedback loop** (quarterly surveys, suggestion box)

**Why it matters:** Builds trust; demonstrates value proposition concretely; engages parents as partners.

---

### 2. Create "Shamila Pedagogy Certification" for Teachers
*Current gap: Teacher development strong but credential absent*

**Structure:**
```
Level 1: Foundations (Year 1)
├─ PSP-inspired structured pedagogy
├─ Values integration techniques
├─ Classroom management + differentiation

Level 2: Mastery (Years 2-3)
├─ Critical thinking facilitation
├─ Project-based learning design
├─ AI literacy + ethical integration

Level 3: Leadership (Years 4+)
├─ Mentor teacher training
├─ Curriculum adaptation
├─ Research contribution
```

**Why it matters:** Attracts and retains top talent; builds institutional knowledge; supports replication.

---

### 3. Launch "Student-Led AI Ethics Council"
*Current gap: AI policy is adult-designed; student ownership limited*

**Process:**
1. **Select 10-12 students** (Grades 10-12) annually
2. **Research** global AI ethics frameworks (UNESCO, EU, Islamic)
3. **Draft** school AI use guidelines for peer feedback
4. **Present** to Advisory Shura Council for input
5. **Publish** annual report on AI use, challenges, recommendations

**Why it matters:** Builds leadership; keeps policy responsive; demonstrates commitment to student voice.

---

### 4. Build "Shamila Impact Dashboard" (Public-Facing)
*Current gap: Metrics exist but not visualized*

**Domains:**
- **Academic**: Bac pass rates, subject mastery trends, university placement
- **Equity**: Scholarship retention, demographic representation, achievement parity
- **Enrichment**: Dimensions participation, portfolio completion, student enterprises
- **Well-being**: Belonging surveys, mental health indicators, parent satisfaction
- **Systemic Impact**: Public-school teachers trained, resources shared, policy contributions

**Platform:** Open-source dashboard (Metabase, Superset) updated quarterly.

**Why it matters:** Attracts mission-aligned investors; proves value; builds public trust.

---

### 5. Formalize "PSP-Shamila Research Partnership"
*Current gap: PSP integration strong but research collaboration underdeveloped*

**Agenda:**
- **Joint research** on "Structured Pedagogy + Values Integration in Moroccan Secondary Schools"
- **Teacher training** co-delivered with Ministry (CRMEF partnership)
- **Open-source resources** (lesson plans, assessment tools) published for public schools
- **Policy papers** advocating for competency-based assessment within Bac framework

**Why it matters:** Positions Shamila as national innovation partner, not isolated private school.

---

## 📈 REVISED SUCCESS METRICS (Year 5)

| Domain | Original Target | Revised Target | Measurement Method |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Regulatory** | 100% compliance + "Excellence" rating | **100% compliance + Ministry recognition** | Inspection reports + awards |
| **Academic** | ≥92% Bac pass; ≥12/20 average | **≥95% Bac pass; ≥12.5/20 average** | National exam results |
| **Enrichment** | ≥80% Dimensions participation | **≥90% participation; ≥50% portfolio completion** | Attendance logs + portfolio records |
| **Equity** | 15% mandatory + 10% aspirational scholarships | **15% mandatory + 15% aspirational**; ≥90% retention | Admissions data + longitudinal tracking |
| **Reputation** | ≥4.5/5 parent satisfaction; 2x waitlist | **≥4.7/5 satisfaction; 3x waitlist** | Annual surveys + admissions data |
| **Systemic Impact** | 50+ teachers trained; 3+ partnerships | **100+ teachers trained; 5+ partnerships; 2 policy contributions** | Training records + MoUs + policy briefs |
| **Financial** | ≥35% margin; endowment growth | **≥35% margin; 5% annual endowment growth** | Audited financial statements |

---

## 🏆 WHAT MAKES THIS MODEL UNIQUELY POWERFUL

```
🌟 Compliance as Foundation, Not Constraint
   Full Bac preparation guaranteed; regulatory risk minimized—brilliant strategic positioning

🌟 Values Integration Through Pedagogy, Not Content Addition
   Quranic reflection, critical thinking, creativity embedded in *how* we teach, not *what* we add

🌟 Enrichment in Non-Core Time
   Friday blocks, after-school clubs, portfolios: no conflict with mandated hours—elegant structural solution

🌟 Parent-Aligned Value Proposition
   "Bac success + character development" meets family priorities while building trust

🌟 Scalable Within Existing Licensing
   No special waivers needed; replicable across Moroccan private school sector

🌟 Systemic Impact Through Resource Sharing
   Teacher training, curriculum resources, assessment tools benefit public system—positions Shamila as national partner
```

---

## 💬 FINAL VERDICT

> **"This is no longer just a school model—it's a template for how private education can serve the public good within existing regulatory frameworks."**

You have successfully transformed a potential obstacle (regulatory constraints) into the **foundation of your strategy**. By working *within* the system rather than against it, you've created:

- **A legally bulletproof structure** that can withstand Ministry scrutiny
- **A philosophically coherent vision** that honors Quranic values, critical consciousness, and future readiness
- **An operationally practical roadmap** that can be implemented with existing licensing
- **A financially sustainable model** that serves equity without compromising viability
- **A systemic impact strategy** that positions your school as a national improvement partner

**The refinements above don't fix what's broken—they unlock what's latent.**

With these enhancements, Al-Madrasa Al-Shamila can become:
- **Morocco's exemplar** of how private schools can innovate within regulation
- **The Ministry's trusted partner** for evidence-based pedagogical improvement
- **A beacon** for values-rooted, future-ready education across the Arab world

---

## 🚀 IMMEDIATE NEXT STEPS (PRIORITIZED)

| Priority | Action | Owner | Timeline |
|----------|--------|-------|----------|
| **1** | Regulatory Deep-Dive + Compliance Officer Hire | Founder + Legal Consultant | Month 1 |
| **2** | Develop "Bac Success + Dimensions" Parent Dashboard prototype | Marketing + Tech Lead | Months 1-2 |
| **3** | Create sample lesson plans for 3 subjects (Math, Arabic, Sciences) showing values integration | Academic Lead + Founding Faculty | Months 2-3 |
| **4** | Draft Student AI Ethics Council charter + recruitment plan | Student Life Dean | Months 3-4 |
| **5** | Build financial model with sensitivity analysis + scholarship sustainability fund | Operations Director + Board | Month 3 |
| **6** | Engage Ministry PSP team for research partnership exploration | Founder + Compliance Officer | Months 3-6 |
| **7** | Pilot Dimensions enrichment program with Grade 10 cohort | Academic Team + Student Life | Year 1, Term 2 |

---

## 🛠️ READY TO ACTIVATE?

I can help you operationalize any of these priorities:

🔹 **Draft the regulatory compliance mapping document** (Arabic/French) with inspection readiness checklist  
🔹 **Design sample lesson plans** showing values integration within official Math, Arabic, and Sciences curricula  
🔹 **Create the "Bac Success + Dimensions" Parent Dashboard prototype** with key metrics and visualizations  
🔹 **Develop Student AI Ethics Council charter** + facilitation guide + recruitment materials  
🔹 **Build the financial model** with sensitivity analysis, scholarship sustainability fund, and endowment strategy  
🔹 **Draft the PSP-Shamila research partnership proposal** for Ministry engagement  

**Which lever shall we pull first?** 🎯

*The vision is clear. The foundation is sound. The path to 9.8/10 is illuminated.* 🌟

---

*Document Version: 2.1 (Assessment Edition) | Last Updated: February 2026*  
*Prepared for: Al-Madrasa Al-Shamila Founding Team* ## Assessment of your “Compliance + Layering” model

### What is strong (keep)

* **Correct strategic posture:** “Compliance + Layering” is the right response if you want a mainstream Moroccan private lycée that remains fully Bac-oriented while differentiating on pedagogy, culture, and enrichment.
* **Good system design:** separating **Official Track (non-negotiable)** from **Enrichment Track (differentiation)** reduces regulatory risk and makes replication easier.
* **Operational clarity:** Friday blocks + after-school clubs are a practical “time container” for innovation without colliding with mandated hours.

### What is weak / risky (must fix)

1. **You state several regulatory points as facts without anchoring them to a text (decree / circular).**

   * Example: “15% mandatory scholarships” reads like *already binding law*. Public reporting indicates **a policy direction / proposed decree** and media reporting varies on details. Treat it as **“expected requirement / proposed measure”** unless you have the actual legal text. ([en.hespress.com][1])
2. **Your Bac benchmarks are wrong/inconsistent.**

   * You wrote “national avg ~75%.” For 2025, the Ministry reported **66.8%** success rate for the *regular session* (enrolled candidates). ([maroc.ma][2])
   * Separate indicator exists for “overall (regular + make-up)” which media reported around **83.3%**. ([Morocco World News][3])
     **Action:** define exactly which denominator you use (enrolled vs independent; regular vs total).
3. **You include compliance claims that appear time-bound or partial.**

   * Fee-increase bans and written contracts were announced as **new measures** in 2025, but you phrase them as stable requirements for “2025–2026.” State them as “current policy measures / enforcement direction,” and keep a compliance watchlist. ([Yabiladi][4])
4. **The model still lacks “inspection-grade evidence design.”**
   Inspectors won’t evaluate your philosophy. They’ll evaluate: timetables, teacher qualifications, assessment records, contracts, fees, safety, and traceable learning evidence.

---

## The 12 high-impact improvements (no fluff)

### A) Make compliance auditable (not just “declared”)

1. **Add a Compliance Matrix (1-page):** requirement → evidence → owner → audit frequency.
   Include: timetable conformity, official textbooks/resources, assessment archives, teacher diplomas, contracts, fee notices, re-enrollment policy, safety protocols. (Your doc currently asserts; it doesn’t “prove.”) ([Yabiladi][4])
2. **Create an “Inspection Pack” structure** (folder tree + templates):

   * `01_Licensing/` `02_Timetables/` `03_Assessments/` `04_TeacherFiles/` `05_ContractsFees/` `06_Scholarships/` `07_SafetyHealth/` `08_ClubsEnrichment/`
3. **Replace “Compliance Officer” with “Compliance + Quality Assurance”** (dual mandate). Compliance alone becomes bureaucratic; QA ties compliance to learning outcomes.

### B) Fix KPIs so they can’t be attacked

4. **Split Bac KPIs into 3 explicit indicators** (you currently mix them):

   * Regular session success rate (enrolled)
   * Total success rate (regular + make-up)
   * Mean score + distribution (≥12/20 etc.)
     Use Ministry-reported baselines (e.g., 2025 regular session 66.8%). ([maroc.ma][2])
5. **Define enrichment KPIs that are measurable without being fake:**

   * Participation (attendance logs)
   * Output (portfolio artifacts count + rubric)
   * Skill growth (pre/post rubrics)
6. **Scholarship KPI:** don’t promise “parity” without methodology. Define: baseline test + tutoring hours + progress bands.

### C) De-risk the pedagogy (“layering” can become chaos)

7. **Standardize “Values Integration” into a 5-minute protocol** (not open-ended):

   * 1 minute: theme statement
   * 2 minutes: prompt
   * 2 minutes: student response method (think-pair-share / quick write)
     This avoids teacher variance and avoids “adding content,” which could be criticized as curriculum drift.
8. **Produce 12 “Model Lessons” across core subjects** (Math, Physics, French, Arabic…) showing compliant delivery + enrichment lens. This becomes your teacher training kernel.
9. **Lock Friday blocks as a governed program**: objectives, safeguarding, attendance, outputs, and “no interference with core hours.” That’s what protects you during inspection.

### D) Reframe AI section to be legally and reputationally safe

10. **Remove any implication that AI affects grading in official track.** Keep AI strictly: literacy, ethics, supervised tool-use, portfolio support.
11. **Write a data minimization policy** for student AI use (parent consent + approved tools list + no student PII).
12. **Academic integrity:** focus on process evidence (drafts, oral defense) rather than “AI detection.” This aligns with your values stance and reduces false accusations.

---

## Corrections you should apply immediately (concrete edits)

### 1) Replace “15% mandatory scholarships” phrasing

Use:

* **“15% free places (policy direction / proposed requirement reported for 2025–2026)”** and add: “subject to final legal text and implementation modalities.” ([en.hespress.com][1])

### 2) Fix Bac baseline statement

Replace:

* “national avg ~75%”
  With one of these (choose and stick to it):
* “2025 regular session (enrolled candidates): 66.8%” ([maroc.ma][2])
* “2025 overall (regular + make-up): ~83.3%” ([Morocco World News][3])

### 3) Qualify the “fee freeze / contracts” requirement

State:

* “Ministry announced measures in 2025 including written contracts with guardians and banning fee increases during the current school year; we will implement these as standard policy and monitor updates.” ([Yabiladi][4])

---

## A tightened “better” executive summary (drop-in replacement)

### Executive Summary (v2.1 — inspection-grade)

**Constraint:** A Moroccan private lycée must deliver the official national curriculum and prepare students for the Moroccan Baccalauréat; differentiation must happen through **pedagogy, enrichment time, and student support**, not through curriculum substitution.

**Model:** **Compliance + Layering**

* **Official Track (100% compliant):** full national curriculum, mandated hours, Bac-format assessment cycle, inspection-ready documentation.
* **Enrichment Track (differentiation, non-core time):** Friday project blocks + after-school clubs + optional portfolio, governed by clear objectives and safeguarding, with values embedded as a *method*, not additional content.

**Why it works:** It is legally low-risk, parent-aligned (“Bac success + character”), operationally replicable, and inspection-defensible (documented timetables, assessments, contracts, fees, scholarship process).

**Outcomes (Year 5):**

* Bac success rate targets specified by denominator (regular session vs overall), measured against Ministry baselines (e.g., 2025 regular session 66.8%). ([maroc.ma][2])
* Enrichment engagement ≥80% (attendance logs + artifact rubrics).
* Scholarship retention ≥85% (with defined academic support protocol).
* Quality assurance: annual compliance audit + learning review cycle.

---

## What you should do next (highest leverage)

1. **Produce the 1-page Compliance Matrix + Inspection Pack templates** (this is your regulatory armor). ([Yabiladi][4])
2. **Define Bac KPI denominators + baselines** (fix credibility). ([maroc.ma][2])
3. **Write 12 model lessons + a 2-hour teacher training module** for the “5-minute values integration protocol.”

If you want, paste your preferred school level focus (collège only, lycée only, or full 6-3-3) and I will output:

* the **Compliance Matrix (table)**,
* the **Inspection Pack folder structure**, and
* the **12 model lesson outline template** in a single consistent format.

[1]: https://en.hespress.com/114701-government-proposes-decree-requiring-private-schools-to-offer-free-education-to-disadvantaged-students.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com   "Government proposes decree requiring private schools to ..."
[2]: https://www.maroc.ma/en/news/2025-baccalaureate-exam-250075-candidates-pass-ordinary-session?utm_source=chatgpt.com   "2025 Baccalaureate Exam : 250075 Candidates Pass ..."
[3]: https://www.moroccoworldnews.com/2025/07/229088/moroccos-baccalaureate-pass-rate-drops-slightly-to-83-3-in-2025/?utm_source=chatgpt.com   "Morocco's Baccalaureate Pass Rate Drops Slightly to 83.3%"
[4]: https://en.yabiladi.com/articles/details/166365/morocco-introduces-regulations-private-schools.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com   "Morocco introduces new regulations for private schools"

content: Designing an educational institution in Morocco with vision and purpose.

I envision a school that harmonizes global academic excellence with local cultural richness, fostering intellectual curiosity and civic responsibility.  
Curriculum would integrate modern pedagogies, multilingual proficiency, and values rooted in Moroccan heritage and universal humanism.  
Every learner would be empowered not just to succeed, but to contribute meaningfully to their community and the world.

content: Investigating curriculum standards and operational guidelines for private schools in Morocco.

I am examining the national education framework to identify authorized academic programs and teaching methodologies permitted within the private sector.  
I am also reviewing regulatory requirements concerning school administration, staffing, enrollment procedures, and compliance with national quality assurance protocols.  
My focus is on ensuring a comprehensive understanding of legal and educational expectations governing private institutions across the country.

Home​
Education systems​
Morocco​

# Primary and secondary education

How is Moroccan primary and secondary education organised? To which Dutch diplomas can you compare Moroccan diplomas?

## Primary and secondary education in Morocco

Pupils aged 6 to 12 attend primary education (*enseignement primaire*) in Morocco.

* Duration

The official length of the full-time programme (not part-time) without study delay.

: 6 years. - Content: reading, writing, social development, Arabic and a foreign language. Pupils complete the education with the *Certificat d’Études Primaires* (CEP)/ شهادة الدراسات الإبتدائية.

Secondary education (*enseignement secondaire*) lasts 6 years. It is divided into 2 phases, each lasting 3 years:

* lower secondary education, concluded with exams for a *Brevet d’Enseignement Collégial* (BEC);
* upper secondary education, concluded with exams for:
  + an Attestation du Baccalauréat if students choose the general education programme of study;
  + a Baccalauréat d’enseignement technologique et professionnel. if students choose the general and vocational education programme of study.

## Attestation du Baccalauréat

Students take exams for an *Attestation du Baccalauréat*if they have chosen the general education programme of study (*enseignement général*). They attend upper secondary general education at a *lycée qualifiant*.

* Duration

The official length of the full-time programme (not part-time) without study delay.

: 3 years (after 3 years of lower secondary education).- Content: the 1st year there is a common curriculum (*tronc commun*). From the 2nd year they follow general education subjects from one of the 3 subject clusters:
  * *lettres modernes* (modern languages);
  * *sciences expérimentales* (natural sciences);
  * *sciences mathématiques* (mathematics).  
    Within these profiles, students can specialise further.
- Admission requirements: a *Brevet d’Enseignement Collégial* (BEC), the lower secondary education diploma.
- Function of the diploma: access to higher education.
- Diploma: *Attestation du Baccalauréat/* شهادة البكالوريا.

We compare an *Attestation du Baccalauréat* to at least a 


HAVO

Senior general secondary education (*hoger algemeen voortgezet onderwijs*, HAVO). A HAVO diploma is awarded upon successful completion of a 5-year programme of general secondary education.

diploma.

Please note: 'at least a HAVO diploma' means that the diploma has a higher level than a HAVO diploma, but a lower level than a 


VWO

Pre-university education (*voorbereidend wetenschappelijk onderwijs*, VWO). A VWO diploma is awarded upon successful completion of a 6-year programme of pre-university education. VWO is the highest level of general secondary education available in the Netherlands.

diploma. The level of the diploma lies between a HAVO diploma and a VWO diploma.

## Baccalauréat d’Enseignement Technologique et Professionnel

Students take exams for a *Baccalauréat d'Enseignement Technologique et Professionnel* (BETP) if they have chosen the general and vocational education programme of study in upper secondary school.

* Duration: 3 years (after 3 years of lower secondary education).
* Content: the 1st year there is a common curriculum (*tronc commun*). From the 2nd year, they follow subjects belonging to the subject cluster they choose. There are 6 subject clusters:
  + *génie civil* (engineering);
  + *génie chimique* (chemical engineering);
  + *génie électrique* (electrical engineering);
  + *génie mécanique* (mechanical engineering);
  + *sciences agronomiques* (agricultural sciences);
  + *sciences économiques* (economics/business administration).  
    These subject clusters consist largely of general education subjects. A third of all teaching is practice-oriented.
* Admission requirements: a *Brevet d’Enseignement Collégial* (BEC), the lower secondary education diploma.
* Function of the diploma: access to higher education.
* Diploma: *Baccalauréat d'Enseignement Technologique et Professionnel*.

We compare a *Baccalauréat d’Enseignement Technologique et Professionnel* to a HAVO diploma with vocational subjects.



## Use of personal data and cookies

We use cookies and process personal data for the following purposes: **Functioneel & Embedded external content**.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Al Akhawayn University in Ifrane.

The **education system in Morocco** comprises pre-school, primary, secondary and tertiary levels. School education is supervised by the Ministry of National Education, with considerable devolution to the regional level. Higher education falls under the Ministry of Higher Education and Executive Training.

School attendance is compulsory up to the age of 13. About 56% of young people are enrolled in secondary education, and 11% are in higher education. The government has launched several policy reviews to improve quality and access to education, and in particular to tackle a continuing problem of illiteracy. Support has been obtained from a number of international organisation such as USAID, UNICEF and the World Bank. A recent report after the new government being formed in 2017 has made Arabic as well as French Compulsory in Public School.

## History

[edit]

A detail of the fountain in the central courtyard Madrasat Al-Attarine (The School of the Spice Traders) in Fes, a *medersa* completed in 1325 under the Marinids.

### Islamic

[edit]

In the year 859, Fatima al-Fihri established Al-Qarawiyiin mosque in Fes, and its associated madrasa is considered by some institutions such as UNESCO and Guinness World Records to be the oldest existing, continually operating higher educational institution in the world.[1][2] The Moroccan historian Mohamed Jabroun (محمد جبرون) wrote that schools in Morocco were first built under the Almohad Caliphate (1121–1269). For centuries, public education in Morocco took place primarily in madrasas and *kuttabs* (كُتَّاب)*—*or as they were called in Morocco, *msids* (مسيد)*.*[3] Education at the *msid* was Islamic and delivered in Arabic.[3] In addition to teaching Quranic exegesis (*tafseer*) and Islamic jurisprudence (*fiqh*), Islamic schools often taught a wide variety of subjects, including literature, science and history.[4] The Marinids founded a number of these schools,[5] including those in Fes, Meknes, and Salé, while the Saadis expanded the Ben Youssef Madrasa in Marrakesh.

### Jewish

[edit]

In the aftermath of the sacking of the *mellah* of Tetuan in the Hispano-Moroccan War, the Alliance Israélite Universelle, a French organization working to empower Jews around the world through a French education, founded its first school in Tetuan in 1862, followed by schools in Tangier (1864), Essaouira (1866), and Asfi (1867), eventually reaching a total of 83 schools—more than all of the AIU's schools in the rest of the world.[6][7][8]

Before the French Protectorate, Jewish girls' schools were teaching reading and writing, as well as vocational workplace training through skills such as tailoring and laundering, and later reading, writing, and shorthand.[7] In 1913, the French authorities replicated this system for Muslim girls in Salé and later other cities.[7]

### French Protectorate

[edit]

Prize distribution at *l'École indigène de Casablanca* in the presence of French ambassador de Saint-Aulaire and director of teaching Gaston Loth.

Following the Treaty of Fes of 1912 and the establishment of the French Protectorate, France established public schools in which education was delivered in French.[3]

Protectorate officials were hesitant about education for general public, afraid that an educated populace would become a source of opposition to the colonial regime.[9] Resident General Hubert Lyautey's elitism shaped the educational system under the French Protectorate, and access to a modern education depended on a student's ethnicity and class, with separate educational opportunities for Europeans, Jews, and Muslims.[9] Only a percentage of the children attending school were Muslim, and of these, most attended the traditional Quranic schools, or *msids*, which were beyond the purview of the Protectorate*.[9]* Most Muslim students studying within the Protectorate's educational system received a vocational education to work in various manual trades.[9]

Five schools for the "sons of notables" were established after 1916 for boys from elite Moroccan families.[9] There were also two *collèges* founded in Fes (1914) and Rabat (1916).[9] These boarding schools, which Susan Gilson Miller likens to Eton College, served to create Lyautey's vision for a class of bicultural Moroccan "gentlemen"—imbued with traditional ideals yet efficient in navigating modern bureaucratic systems—to fill roles in the country's administration.[9]

In 1920, Lyautey founded the *Institut des Hautes Études Marocaines*, located within the *Bibliothèque Générale* next to the colonial headquarters in Rabat, for colonial administrators who wanted to learn more about Moroccan history and society.[9]

After 1920, French *instituteurs*, who were rigorously prepared in an Amazigh dialect and trained as teachers and intelligence gatherers, were assigned to schools in rural areas for speakers of Amazigh languages.[9] The *Collège d'Azrou*, one of these schools that was developed into a *collège*, was one of the instruments for the implementation of a Berber Dhahīr[9] and to "help form an Amazigh elite that would help France implement its divide and rule policies."[10] It trained its Amazigh students for high roles in the colonial administration, as well as forming "a Berber elite steeped in French culture, in an environment where Arabic influences were rigorously excluded."[9][11] This hierarchical educational system was designed to "guarantee an indigenous elite loyal to France and ready to enter its service," though its permeability in practice made it a "vehicle of social mobility."[9]

The inadequacies of traditional education at the *msid* became apparent, and private Moroccan citizens, particularly of the bourgeois merchant class, started to found what would later become known as "free schools"—with modernized curricula and instruction in Arabic—independently of each other.[3] The teaching quality at these free schools varied greatly, from "uninspiring fqîhs, scarcely removed from their *msîds*" to "the country's most distinguished and imaginative *'ulamâ*."[3] Many of the young teachers in Fes were simultaneously taking classes at al-Qarawiyyin University, and several would go on to lead the Moroccan Nationalist Movement.[3]

### Independence

[edit]

In the 44-year period of the French Protectorate, a total of only 1415 Moroccans had earned baccalaureate diplomas, 640 of whom were Muslims and 775 of whom were Jews.[9] Additionally, under the Protectorate, only 15% of school-age children were in school.[9] It was necessary to develop the educational system and a university network out of the limited resources left by the French in order to face the challenges awaiting the newly independent nation-state, such as "creating and consolidating state institutions, building a national economy, organizing civil society and the nascent political parties, [and] establishing social protections for a needy population."[9]

Arabization became an imperative. Ahmed Boukmakh published *Iqra'* (اِقْرَأ, "*Read*"), the first series of Arabic textbooks in Morocco, in 1956, 1957, and 1958.[12][13] In 1957, Muhammad Al-Fasi attempted to Arabize first grade, but this attempt was not successful.[14] The Institute for Studies and Research on Arabization was established in 1960 and the first Congress of Arabization was held in Rabat in 1961.[14][15]

The Cultural Agreement of 1957, signed by the Moroccan and French governments, allowed for the continuation of French education in Morocco administered by a body known as the (which was replaced by AEFE in 1990).[16][17]

In 1958, Muhammad al-Fasi's successor, established a blueprint for education reform in four goals:

1. unify the traditional and modern systems
2. Arabize all subjects
3. generalize literacy
4. Moroccanize, i.e. train nationals to replace foreign teachers[14]

It was in 1963 that education was made compulsory for all Moroccan children between the ages of 6 through 13[18] and during this time all subjects were Arabized in the first and second grades, while French was maintained as the language of instruction of maths and science in both primary and secondary levels.

In 1965, demanding the right to public higher education for Moroccans, the National Union of Moroccan Students (الاتحاد الوطني لطلبة المغرب, *UNEM*) organized a march starting from Muhammad V Secondary School in Casablanca, which devolved the following day into the 1965 Moroccan riots.[19] King Hassan II blamed the events on teachers and parents, and declared: "Allow me to tell you that there is no greater danger to the State than a pseudo-intellectual, and you are pseudo-intellectuals. It would have been better if you were all illiterate.”[20][21] In the aftermath, teachers were persecuted.[22]

Under Hassan II, the Institute of Sociology (معهد السوسيولوجيا) run by Abdelkebir Khatibi was dissolved in 1970.[23] In 1973, the humanities at government-controlled universities were Arabized, in which the Istiqlal leader Allal al-Fassi played a major role,[24] and curricula were substantially changed. Arabization was instrumentalized to suppress critical thought,[23] a move which Dr. Susan Gilson Miller described as a "crude and obvious attempt to foster a more conservative atmosphere within academia and to dampen enthusiasm for the radicalizing influences filtering in from Europe."[25] The Minister of Education Azzeddine Laraki, following a report conducted by a committee of four Egyptians including two from al-Azhar University, replaced sociology with Islamic thought in 1983.[23]

To meet the rising demand for secondary education in the 1970s, Morocco imported French speaking teachers from countries such as France, Romania, and Bulgaria to teach maths and sciences, and Arab teachers to teach humanities and social studies.

A 1973 article in *Lamalif* described Moroccan society as broken and fragmented, with misdirected energy from a discouraged and ashamed elite contributing to low standards of education.[26]

By 1989, Arabization of all subjects across all grades in both primary and secondary public education was accomplished. However, French remained the medium of instruction for scientific subjects in technical and professional secondary schools, technical institutes and universities.[27]

The government has undertaken several reforms to improve the access of education and reduce regional differences in the provision of education. In 1999, King Mohammed VI announced the National Charter for Education and Training (الميثاق الوطني للتربية و التكوين).[28][29] In the same year, a committee for education was established with the goal of reforming the educational system in Morocco.[30] On July 15, 2002, decree number 2.02.382 established the Ministry of National Education, Early Education, and Athletics.[30]

The King announced the period between 1999 and 2009 as the "Education Decade." During this time the government's reform initiative focused on five main themes to facilitate the role of knowledge in economic development; the key themes were education, governance, private sector development, e-commerce and access. The World Bank and other multilateral agencies have helped Morocco to improve the basic education system.

Under Said Amzazi, Morocco passed the framework-law 51.17 summer 2019.[31]

### French schools

[edit]

To this day, French schools—which are colloquially referred to as "*la mission*," whether they're actually related to the Mission Laïque Française or not—still have a major presence in Morocco. In these schools, French is the language of instruction, and Arabic is only taught as a second language if at all.[32]

The schools are certified by the Agency for French Education Abroad, and administered by the French Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and the student body is typically composed of the children of Morocco's elite and well-to-do classes.[32] These schools, such as Lycée Lyautey in Casablanca, are typically located in big cities such as Casablanca, Rabat, Fes, Marrakesh, Meknes, and Oujda.[33]

France is the number 1 destination for Moroccan students leaving the country to study abroad, receiving 57.7% of all Moroccans studying outside of Morocco. Moroccan students also represent the largest group of foreign students in France, at 11.7% of all international students at universities in France, according to a 2015 UNESCO study.[34]

## Structure of the education system

[edit]

The education system in Morocco comprises pre-school, primary, secondary and tertiary levels. Government efforts to increase the availability of education services have led to increased access at all levels of education. Morocco's education system consists of 6 years of primary, 3 years of lower-middle / intermediate school, 3 years of upper secondary, and tertiary education.

The education system is under the purview of the Ministry of National Education (MNE) and Ministry of Higher Education and Executive Training. The Ministry of National Education decentralized its functions to regional levels created in 1999 when 72 provinces were subsumed into 12 regional administrative units. Then the responsibility of the provision of education services has been slowly devolving to the regional level. This decentralization process will ensure that education programs are responsive to regional needs and the budget is administered locally. Each region has a Regional Academy for Education and Training and a regional director who is senior to provincial delegates within the region. The regional academies will also be responsible for developing 30 percent of the curriculum so that it is locally relevant. The central level of the MNE continues to manage the other 70 percent.[35] Also the delegations are charged with providing services for education in their regions.[36]

### Pre-school

[edit]

According to the National Charter, preprimary education is compulsory and available to all children under the age of 6. This level is open to children of ages 4–6 years old. There are two types of pre-primary schools in Morocco: kindergarten and Quranic schools. The kindergarten is a private school that provides education mainly in cities and towns; the Quranic schools prepare children for primary education by helping them develop basic literacy and numeracy skills. Quranic schools have the potential to become a major force in the fight against illiteracy. (timss)with approximately 80 percent of all children attending some form of Quranic school for some portion of their school years.[35] In 2007 the gross enrollment rate of pre-primary students in Morocco was about 60 percent, with the GER of males being 69.4 percent and that for females 49.6 percent. The GER for females have been increasing since the past few years and for the males it has been about 69 percent since 2003.[37]

### Primary

[edit]

The primary education consists of 6 years for children of ages 6–12 years old. Students are required to pass Certificat d'etudes primaires to be eligible for admission in lower secondary schools.[36]

The gross enrollment rates (GER) at the primary level have been consistently rising in the 2000s. In 2007 the total GER at the primary level was 107.4 percent, with 112 percent for males and 101 percent for females. But the Gender Parity Index for GER was 0.89, which shows that the issue of gender inequality persists at the primary level. The repetition rate at the primary level is 11.8 percent; the repetition rate for males at the primary level is 13.7 percent and for females it is 9.7 percent and the rates are declining for the past few years for both genders. The dropout rate at the primary level in 2006 was 22 percent, and is slightly higher for girls than boys, at 22 and 21 percent respectively.[37] The dropout rates have been falling since 2003, but is still very high compared to other Arab countries, such as Algeria, Oman, Egypt and Tunisia.[38]

### Secondary

[edit]

There are three years of lower-middle school. This type of education is provided through what is referred to as the *Collège*. After 9 years of basic education, students begin upper secondary school and take a one-year common core curriculum, which is either in arts or science. First year students take arts and or science, mathematics or original education. Second year students take earth and life sciences, physics, agricultural science, technical studies or are in A or B mathematics track.

The gross enrollment rate (GER) at the secondary level in 2007 was 55.8 percent. But in secondary education the grade repetition and drop-out rates especially remain high.[39] The gender parity index for GER at secondary level was 0.86 in 2007; it is not better than other Arab countries and reflects considerable disparity between genders in enrollment at the secondary level.

### Tertiary

[edit]

Source:[40]

The higher education system consists of both private and public institutes. The country has fourteen major public universities,[36] including Mohammed V University in Rabat and Al-Karaouine University, Fes,[41] along with specialist schools, such as the Al-Zahrawi International University of Health Sciences,[42] and the music conservatories of Morocco supported by the Ministry of Culture. The Karaouine University at Fes has been teaching since 859, making it the world's oldest continuously operating university.[43] However, there are dozens of national and international universities like SIST British University that work along with the public universities to improve the higher education in Morocco. In addition, there are a large number of private universities. The total number of graduates at the tertiary level in 2007 was 88,137: the gross enrollment rate at the tertiary level is 11 percent and it has not fluctuated significantly in the past few years.[38] Admission to public universities requires only a baccalauréat, whereas admission to other higher public education, such as engineering school, require competitive special tests and special training before the exams.

Another growing field apart from engineering and medicine is business management. According to the Ministry of Education the enrollment in Business Management increased by 3.1 percent in the year 2003-04 when compared to preceding year 2002–2003. Generally, an undergraduate business degree requires three years and an average of two years for master's degree.[44]

Universities in Morocco have also started to incorporate the use of information and communication technology. A number of universities have started providing software and hardware engineering courses as well; annually the academic sector produces 2,000 graduates in the field of information and communication technologies.[45]

Moroccan institutions have established partnerships with institutes in Europe and Canada and offer joint degree programs in various fields from well-known universities.[44]

To increase public accountability, the Moroccan universities have been evaluated since 2000, with the intention of making the results public to all stakeholders, including parents and students.[27]

## Results in the education sector

[edit]

### Private higher education institutes

[edit]

Despite having a number of private institutes the enrollment in private higher education institutes is still low, less than 3.5 percent of total university population. Private institutes also suffer from less qualified or inadequate staff. This is primarily due to inhibiting tuition costs. Curriculum of especially the business schools is outdated and needs to be revised according to the changing demands of the labor market. Private sector companies also do not make sufficient contribution in providing working knowledge to professional institutes of the current business environment.[44]

### Access to school education

[edit]

Internal efficiency is also low with high dropout and repetition rates. There is also an unmet need of rising demand of middle schools after achieving high access rates in primary education.[46] The problem is more acute in the rural schools due to inadequate supply and quality of instructional materials. The poor quality of education becomes an even greater problem due to Arabic-Berber language issues: most of the children from Berber families hardly know any Arabic, which is the medium of instruction in schools, when they enter primary level.[35]

### Literacy

[edit]

The Maghreb region has high rates of literacy. In Morocco, the adult literacy rate is still high at around 74 percent in 2018, thanks to concerted efforts being made since independence in 1956 to reduce the rate of illiteracy which at that time was 32 percent. Morocco is the 16 most literate in the Arab countries.

### Immigration

[edit]

There has also been a high immigration rate of skilled workers, that is, the proportion of highly skilled emigrants among educated people is high. In this way Morocco is losing a substantial proportion of its skilled work force to foreign countries, forming the largest migrant population among North Africans in Europe.[27]

## Reform efforts

[edit]

### Literacy

[edit]

UIS Literacy Rate Morocco population above 15 years of age 1980-2015

Since the late 1980s the Maghreb countries’ governments have been partnering with civil society organizations to fight illiteracy. The NGO Programme launched in 1988 delivers literacy to 54% of all learners enrolled in adult literacy programmes. Ministerial and General Programme also focus on various ministries and community to deliver literacy programmes. In-Company programmes cater to the needs of the working population focused on continuous in-company training.[47] Morocco and other Maghreb countries are now fully committed to eradicate illiteracy. Morocco officially adopted its National Literacy and Non-formal Education Strategy in 2004. An integrated vision of literacy, development and poverty reduction was promoted by National Initiative for Human Development (INDH), launched by the King Mohammed VI in May 2005.[47]

### Government reviews

[edit]

Improving the quality of outcomes in the education sector has become a key priority for Morocco's government. A comprehensive renovation of the education and training system was developed in a participatory manner in 1998–99, which led to the vision for long-term expansion of this sector in response to the country's social and economic development requirements.[48] The outcome was the promulgation of the 1999 National Education and Training Charter (CNEF). The CNEF, with strong national consensus, declared 2000-2009 the decade for education and training, and established education and training as a national priority, second only to territorial integrity. The reform program, as laid out by the CNEF, also received strong support from the donor community. Nevertheless, during the course of implementation, the reform program encountered delays.

In 2005 the Moroccan government adopted a strategy with the objective of making ICT accessible in all public schools to improve the quality of teaching; infrastructure, teacher training and the development of pedagogical content was also part of this national programme.[45] In August 2019, the government passed a law to commence reforms on education including teaching science subjects in French language.[49] In 2021, many series of campaign were done by the Moroccans to remove the French language and to replace it with the English language as a secondary language.[50][51][52][53] Which later was announced no change would be made regarding the French language.[54]

### External bodies

[edit]

A number of donors including USAID and UNICEF are implementing programs to improve the quality of education at the basic level and to provide training to teachers. The World Bank also provides assistance in infrastructure upgrades for all levels of education and offer skill development trainings and integrated employment creation strategies to various stakeholders.[35] At the request of the Government's highest authorities, a bold Education Emergency Plan (EEP) was drawn up to catch up on this reform process. The EEP, spanning the period 2009–12, draws on the lessons learned during the last decade. In this context, the Government requested five major donors (European Union (EU), European Investment Bank (EIB), Agence française de développement (AFD), African Development Bank (AfDB) and the World Bank) to assist the implementation of the EEP reform agenda.[55]

## Curriculum

Within the Primary section of the school, we follow the Cambridge International Primary Curriculum for various subjects:

## Art & Design

Students explore various artistic techniques and mediums, developing creativity and visual literacy through drawing, painting, and sculpture.

## Digital Literacy (ICT)

Focuses on basic computer skills, internet safety, and introductory coding, preparing students for the digital world.

## English

Enhances reading, writing, speaking, and listening skills, fostering appreciation for literature and effective communication.

## ESL

Supports non-native speakers in building English proficiency through targeted listening, speaking, reading, and writing activities.

## Mathematics

Develops problem-solving and analytical skills through engaging activities in number systems, geometry, algebra, and data handling.

## Music

Encourages exploration of music through singing, instruments, and listening, fostering creativity and cultural appreciation.

## Physical Education

Promotes physical fitness and teamwork through various activities, encouraging a healthy lifestyle.

## Science

Fosters curiosity and critical thinking with hands-on experiments in biology, chemistry, physics, and earth science.

## Staff

Staff will also use their experience of the UK educational setting by blending in key areas of the National Curriculum (England) – this especially true in Year 6 as we prepare our students for life in The Secondary School.

Teacher Profiles

## ADDITIONAL SUBJECT AREAS

Given the importance of a wide and balanced curriculum, we also provide Humanities lessons, and for this, we follow the requirements of the National Curriculum (England).

Subjects include History, Geography and aspects of Global Perspectives curriculum. For French and Arabic, our curriculum is based on the requirements of the Moroccan National Curriculum. These are bespoke to the school and were approved by the ISI inspectorate in 2023.

Finally, the school will be introducing Robotics & Computer Coding as a stand-alone subject for the 2024 – 2025 academic period. This is especially exciting given the ever increasing importance of this subject in our rapidly changing World.
